data.title: JBS: Meat In The U.S. Has Never Been So Expensive, And This Company Can Help Pay The Bill
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my recommendation to buy JBS (JBS) shares. This article is part of my initial coverage thesis published on June 23, 2024, and my last article about the company was published on May 16, 2025. The price of beef in the US is high, and a movement in the American Congress could severely impact JBS's business and the price of its shares. My intention in this article is to address this topic in the context of my investment thesis.

Context

It is worth noting that in June 2025, JBS completes its listing on the American market. Previously, the company had its shares listed in Brazil and American Depositary Receipt on the NYSE, and after the reorganization, the company listed its shares on the NYSE and Brazilian Depositary Receipts on the Brazilian Stock Exchange, known as B3. The objective of the operation was to expand investment capacity and adapt the capital structure to the global profile.

Although I have no articles published on Seeking Alpha after this new listing, I created articles in 2024 and 2025 when the company still had American Depositary Receipts. My buy recommendations yielded gains for investors.

My objective in this article is to address a new and major risk for the company. But first, I'll revisit the investment thesis and the reasons that made me recommend buying the shares in the past.

Revisiting the Investment Thesis

In general, the arguments I cited were that JBS was the largest animal protein company in the world. Although exposure to a commodity brings volatility, the company mitigates this with its global diversification. At the same time, the company had the best financial indicators compared to its competitors; however, its valuation multiple was the lowest. Therefore, I saw this as an asymmetry.

It is worth highlighting that since then there have been few changes, and the drivers of the investment thesis remain intact. Despite the listing on the NYSE and greater international visibility (which should reduce the discount to peers), there was little change in the company's pricing.

Financial Analysis and Valuation

As a reference, we can see below in a comparison against other peers that JBS shares have the best performance in the last 12 months. Additionally, the company has good EBITDA margin and Return on Equity numbers, however, the company still has the most depressed multiples compared to its competitors.

Therefore, I remain optimistic about JBS's investment thesis. The average EV/EBITDA of the peers is 8.5x, but if JBS only reaches the multiple of the Brazilian peer MBRF Global Foods (MBRFY) (7.2x EBITDA), it would already represent an upside of 22%, which corroborates my recommendation to buy.

Latest Results

It is worth noting that the tailwind for the company's business is very strong, and as we can see below, the company has published results above expectations for 6 consecutive quarters.

The next results will be released on March 25th. For Q4 results, I estimate that the company should report revenue of BRL 123 billion ($23.8 billion), an annual increase of 5%. Supply bottlenecks, however, should cause the company to report an EBITDA margin of 7.0% (-230 bps YoY and -110 bps QoQ).

The operation in the United States, especially beef, is expected to remain under pressure, with lower margins compared to 3Q25. The segment that should stand out the most positively is chicken exports to China and the European Union.

In my opinion, the Brazilian beef operation will also be weaker sequentially, as spreads come under pressure due to higher cattle prices and lower average export prices in the quarter. On the other hand, pork margins (less relevant) should remain broadly stable.

In short, the results should be slightly weaker than in the last quarter; however, one of the company's greatest competitive advantages should once again stand out: its diversification of operations and geographies.

Despite all the positive points I mentioned, there must be reasons for the discount in valuation compared to competitors, and I want to address the risk that I believe is mainly responsible for this below.

Breakdown of Meatpackers in the US?

It is a fact that Americans have never paid so much to eat beef; an action by the Democratic Party wants part of the bill to be transferred to the industry. According to The Wall Street Journal, Democratic senators are preparing a bill to break up large meatpacking companies operating in the country and limit their market share.

Additionally, the project should investigate companies with foreign capital, such as JBS. The news impacted the share prices of JBS, Tyson Foods (TSN), and MBRF. At a time when the US has its smallest cattle herd in 75 years and when demand for meat remains high, the end consumer is paying increasingly more.

The nominal price of beef has risen since 2021 and is at the highest level in history, data from the St. Louis Fed show, while the inflation-adjusted price is the highest since the early 1980s. The product has put pressure on inflation and bothered Republicans and Democrats.

Since assuming the presidency for the second time, President Trump has already opened an investigation against companies in the sector and asked producers to lower cattle prices. The American president also ordered the expansion of meat imports from Argentina but has not yet managed to reverse the rise in prices.

In detail, the proposal foresees, for example, the separation of beef production units from large companies in the sector, which would hit JBS hard. Furthermore, the project includes the establishment of market concentration limits at the national and regional levels, which, if exceeded by companies, will result in compulsory divestment.

There is also, for foreign companies like JBS, the threat of a fine-toothed comb in their operations in the US, but without details on what the legislators are trying to find.

It is worth noting that companies like JBS point to the high prices charged by producers as the cause of the problem, which is even leading to the closure of production plants, such as one in Nebraska recently closed by Tyson.

Well, as an investment analyst, I will follow this case closely and will bring news in a future article. I believe there is little chance of the process being approved; however, this could be a reason for the Trump administration to create new investigations into the company.

The Bottom Line

Since my last article about JBS, some important events have occurred. The company officially listed its shares on the NYSE, which should reduce the valuation discount compared to other companies. However, this has not occurred to date.

In parallel, a new risk emerged: the possibility of the breakup of meatpackers in the USA. This would be an initiative to reduce beef prices, a situation that has impacted inflation. I will follow the developments in this case and bring you news, but at the moment, this is not a reason for me to downgrade my recommendation.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy JBS shares. The upside of more than 20%, based on a multiple analysis, makes the risk/return ratio on the investment very attractive in my opinion.

data.title: Merger With Northfield Bancorp And Second-Step Conversion Create Opportunity For Columbia Financial
data.description:
On February 2, Columbia Financial (CLBK) announced its intent to acquire Northfield Bancorp (NFBK). Valued at just under $600 million, the deal could create one of the three largest regional banks headquartered in New Jersey. The transaction is being done in conjunction with a second-step conversion where CLBK will transition to a fully public stock holding company. The deal is expected to be completed by the third quarter of this year.

Once Columbia Financial and Northfield are combined, their total assets will equal roughly $18 billion. Columbia currently operates 71 bank branches in New Jersey. Northfield controls 17 bank locations in New Jersey and an additional 20 in New York. A map of the potential combined service areas for the two companies can be seen below.

While the addition of the Northfield assets certainly has value for CLBK shareholders, the second-step conversion may unlock some additional potential for the company as well. The offering for new Columbia shares is expected to raise proceeds of $1.4 billion to $1.9 billion that it can use for loan growth, initiation of a dividend, and restructuring of its securities portfolio. I think CLBK may be worth initiating a small position ahead of the conclusion of the merger for the reasons discussed here.

Columbia Financial Company Overview

Columbia Financial, in its current form, is a holding company for Columbia Bank. It is considered a partial public entity as it is majority owned by Columbia Bank Mutual Holding Company. The “first step conversion” for the company took place back in 2018. CLBK currently has a market capitalization of $1.91 billion.

At the end of 2025, Columbia Financial reported control of $11.0 billion in total assets, an increase of 5.2% from the end of 2024. Most of that increase was the result of a larger loan portfolio that was valued at $8.2 billion once allowances for credit losses are considered. The growth in the loan book over the last 12 months can be seen below.

Residential mortgages and commercial real estate take up the bulk of the loan mix for Columbia Bank at 31.0% and 30.5% of the total gross loans, respectively. About two-thirds of that commercial real estate portfolio is allocated toward non-owner-occupied CRE. The next largest loan segment for the bank belongs to multifamily real estate at 20.4%.

The company was able to grow its deposits by 4.3% last year to reach a total of just over $8.4 billion by the end of December. Almost 18% of those were held in non-interest-bearing accounts. Just over a third of the bank’s deposits are held in time accounts that pay a higher interest rate and push the total cost of deposits for the bank just above the national average for its peers, which was 2.12% in Q4 2025.

Columbia Financial currently does not pay a dividend. However, it has announced its intent to begin distributions once the merger with Northfield is completed. The exact amount of that dividend has yet to be determined. With its profits expected for FY 2026, a 20% payout ratio would give CLBK a yield of just under 1%.

Analysis of Columbia Financial

Columbia Financial reported earnings of $0.15 per diluted share in Q4 2025 and $0.51 per share in FY 2025. The company reported a loss in FY 2024 due to balance sheet restructuring, which makes some year-over-year comparisons less meaningful. Other highlights for CLBK from Q4 included the following:

  • Loans receivable increased by $375.1 million, or 4.7%, when compared to the end of the previous year.
  • Net interest margin increased by 48 basis points to 2.36% when compared to Q4 2024.
  • Net interest income rose by 29.7% year-over-year in Q4 to $60.2 million.
  • Non-interest expenses were up slightly in Q4 2025 when compared to the same quarter in 2024, but for the full year, they actually decreased by 0.2%.

Below is a chart showing other financial metrics for Columbia Financial at the end of Q4. Many of these results, including return on assets and return on equity, are well below where I would like to see them in a strong small-cap regional bank. ROA of 0.57% and ROE of 5.43% are well below the national average for similar-sized banks, which stood at 1.35% and 12.30% at the end of Q4. Despite its improvements, net interest margin is also still well below the national mean of 3.89%.

Fortunately, asset quality for Columbia Financial appears to be strong, even if slight deterioration was apparent in the final quarter of last year. CLBK had a non-performing loan ratio of 0.46% and a non-performing asset ratio of 0.34%. These numbers were six and four basis points higher than the results from Q3, but the bank appears to have ample coverage for future losses based on the data below. Net charge offs in Q4 were only 0.03% after standing at 0.04% in Q3 and 0.07% at the end of 2024.

If this were an analysis of Columbia Financial alone, I think the highest rating that I could give its stock is a hold. While trends are moving in the right direction, many of these numbers discussed here are just too far below the norm. However, the acquisition of Northfield Bancorp looks like a solid decision that will boost the performance of CLBK moving forward.

Analysis of Northfield Bancorp

Northfield Bancorp, the holding company for Northfield Bank, released its 2025 10-K on March 2. The company announced that its annual earnings of $0.02 per diluted share were impacted by a $1.03 per share non-cash charge against its goodwill. Without that discount, the profit of $1.05 per share would have been 45.8% higher than what was earned the previous year. I do like the fact that the company made the move to wipe its goodwill slate clean heading into this year.

The company reported assets of $5.8 billion that included a net loan balance of $3.8 billion. Total loans for the bank have been on a slight decline for the last two years. An overview of the company and its key financial metrics at the end of 2025 is shown below.

As a standalone company, NFBK is expected to earn $1.35 per share in FY 2026. That would be a 28.6% increase from FY 2025 without consideration of the goodwill charge. The company has a forward PE below 10. At its current share price of $13.31, even with the run-up related to the merger announcement at the start of February, NFBK is still trading well below its tangible book value of $16.31 per share. S&P Global highlighted the company in an article on March 4 as its discount to its TBV is one of the largest for all publicly traded banks in the US.

From my perspective, the deal for Northfield makes CLBK look like a winner, even without the stock conversion. The new addition is expected to be roughly 50% accretive to FY 2027 earnings. Columbia Financial expects to earn back its 4.4% TBV dilution in 1.8 years. Acquiring Northfield in this manner will allow CLBK to achieve a normalized return on equity faster than it would on a standalone basis. It will also get Columbia Bank’s regulatory CRE ratio below the 300% threshold.

Other benefits from the transaction are shown below. Columbia Financial will get a significant boost to its return on assets in FY 2027, moving that number above the 1% mark that I prefer to see in the banks that I normally recommend. Its efficiency ratio will drop below 50%, well under the mean for a bank of its size.

Risks to Consider

As with any planned acquisition, there is always the risk that the deal will not go through as planned. While the current regulatory environment for bank mergers at the federal level has been quite supportive, this deal has an added layer of complexity with the conversion attached. Even if everything proceeds on schedule, the two companies may not be able to achieve the expected synergies and cost savings. There may also be customers for both banks who choose this merger as an opportunity to move their accounts to another institution, as the market in and around northern New Jersey and New York City is extremely competitive.

The limited geographic footprint for the bank certainly presents some limitations for its operations. Columbia Financial is largely dependent on the economy in the state of New Jersey and the area around New York City for its overall success. The new mayor in New York City is expected to be much less friendly to businesses and may move to increasing property tax rates in the city.

The addition of the Northfield Bank assets does expose CLBK to some added risk with its rent-regulated multifamily loans. Currently, just over 60% of Northfield's entire loan portfolio is comprised of multifamily loans. The total allocated to rent-regulated properties is 10.5%. Once the two companies are combined, Columbia Financial's exposure will be just 3.3%, but this is still an area worth watching for potential signs of deterioration.

Like most all regional banks, Columbia Financial is also subject to a slowdown in new loan activity if the overall US economy takes a turn south later this year. Inflation may keep interest rates higher than expected for the next several quarters, and there is some uncertainty for the financial sector around the appointment of a new Fed chair. The recent jobs report from March 6 did little to support the idea that the US economy is on solid footing. As part of the company’s annual report, Northfield Bancorp showed the unemployment rate in each of its service areas at the end of last year and how it rose in five of the six counties served by the bank. In the only county it stayed flat, King County, NY, it was still well above the national average.

Conclusion

There are a lot of moving parts to the story of Columbia Financial. If I were only analyzing the company without the merger, I do not think there is enough to rate its stock as a buy. However, with the acquisition of Northfield, which appears to be taking place at a very reasonable valuation, and the second-step conversion, which will make the stock more attractive to other investors, I think CLBK is worth considering for a portfolio that is looking for small-cap bank ideas.

As it stands now, Northfield is trading at a slight discount to its takeover price of $14.25 per share. Investors who are willing to take on the added arbitrage risk, can actually buy Columbia Financial shares at a modest discount through a purchase of NFBK, which is why I consider its stock to be a buy at the current time as well.

data.title: What's Driving The Gold Price? ... And Other Important Questions
data.description:
Gold has been treasured by people across the globe for thousands of years. It’s been used as currency and for bartering, as a symbol of wealth, and more recently for its investment characteristics. This article answers some of the most common questions from investors. What’s driving the gold price? Does gold offer any protection, and if so, against what? Is it potentially suitable for investors with environmental or social concerns, and how can investors use it in their portfolio?

Today, gold is held by different types of investors for a variety of reasons. For example, wealth managers may use it to help cushion losses in their client portfolios if stock markets fall. Individuals might want to invest in gold if they’re looking for physical assets that may retain their value over the long term even after inflation. And while central banks are not really investors, they often hold gold as part of their reserves, alongside other assets including foreign currency and government-issued securities such as US Treasury bonds. Central banks hold these assets to help them manage liquidity in the financial system and the economy, especially in times of crisis.

Gold held by retail and institutional investors has increased substantially in recent decades, due largely to gold-backed exchange-traded products (ETPs) making it easier for them to gain exposure.

Total amount of gold held by gold-backed exchange-traded products (ETPs)

Q1: What’s been driving the gold price?

Gold has been among the best-performing assets of 2026 so far, just as it was in 2025 and 2024. It has at times outpaced the returns from US equities.1 The gold price reached an all-time high of almost $5,600 per ounce in late January. Silver similarly broke above $120 per ounce. Of course, nobody can predict the future with any certainty, but we can comment on recent activity and try to explain what we believe has been behind the phenomenal rise in this precious metal.

What makes gold so interesting from an investment perspective is that its price can be influenced by a variety of factors. Some of these are based on macroeconomic principles, and others are more psychological. Gold’s rise in recent years has been partly driven by central banks. Those in developed markets have been cutting their short-term interest rates, which tends to be positive for gold, while those in emerging markets have been major buyers of gold, making this sector a key source of demand for the precious metal. Overall, central banks continued to buy large amounts of gold for their reserves in 2025 as they did the previous three years.2

The inflation picture has also supported gold. Lower inflation expectations have helped push down medium-term bond yields, which reduces the opportunity cost of holding gold. Let’s explain. Because gold doesn’t pay any income, it’s less attractive when investors are able to get a high income from other assets that are considered to have lower risks, such as government-issued bonds. Conversely, when bond yields go down, gold typically becomes more attractive.

The relative value of the US dollar (USD) can often have a material effect on the price of gold. The USD has weakened considerably in 2025, which has made gold less expensive for non-USD investors and other consumers. This was the case for European investors recently when the euro (EUR) rose to its strongest level versus the USD in over four years.3

The psychological influences are linked to the perceptions that, in volatile times, gold tends to be relatively stable compared to equities and other risky assets and is often sought after during periods of heightened fear and uncertainty. It’s commonly understood that central banks, especially in emerging markets, have been buying gold for potentially a variety of reasons. These include wanting to diversify their balance sheets away from USD assets while also helping them manage geopolitical and economic risks. Other investors have also been buying gold, whether directly through the purchase of gold coins and small bars or via physical gold ETPs.

Q2: Does gold offer any protection from adverse events?

The gold price over the past year has also been driven by increased geopolitical and economic uncertainty as well as stock market volatility. More accurately, the price has been driven largely by investors buying gold because of these increased risks and volatility. If we look at the physical gold ETP market for the past five years, as seen in the chart above, we can see that investor demand was strongest during two distinct periods. Those are the first months of the pandemic, which started in March of 2020, and when Russia invaded Ukraine in February of 2022. In both cases, there was a high amount of uncertainty and heightened volatility.

It’s important to highlight that gold doesn’t provide any actual “protection” in the sense that there are no guarantees. As with other investments, the price of gold can go down as well as up, and investors may not get back the amount invested.

Historical evidence suggests that gold has often been able to provide a “cushion” against the downside risks that uncertainty and volatility can inflict on an equity portfolio. Gold has also tended to hold up during sudden rises in inflation or during periods of stagflation, which is a scenario whereby an economy suffers high inflation, high unemployment, and low growth all at the same time.4

Over the past 50 years, gold has been one of the only asset classes that has demonstrated the tendency to move in the same direction as both the level of inflation and the change in inflation. In other words, the gold price has tended to rise when inflation is both higher than normal and rising.

Q3: Is gold an ethical investment?

The gold mining and refining industries are focused on improving sustainability, accountability, and transparency. The goal is to provide investors and consumers with more clarity and confidence that the gold they are buying has been sourced and processed according to a high set of standards. The World Gold Council launched the Conflict-Free Gold Standard (CFGS) in 2012, putting in place processes for mining companies to guard against the risk that gold would be used to fund or support unlawful armed conflict. The CFGS has now been incorporated in the Council’s more comprehensive Responsible Gold Mining Principles, issued in 2019, for its 32 member firms (mining companies). This framework establishes what constitutes responsible gold mining.

Likewise, the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) has a mandatory Responsible Sourcing program for any refiner wanting to trade with the London Bullion Market. The LBMA issued its Responsible Gold Guidance in 2012 and has updated the document multiple times since then to incorporate incrementally improving guidelines and auditing guidance around relevant issues. They continue working with mining companies to develop best practices across the industry.

The World Gold Council and LBMA have been issuing separate guidance to their members since 2012 and are now working together on a combined framework, trialling a blockchain solution for tracking gold from the mine all the way through the supply chain.

Mining is an energy-intensive process that often impacts the local ecology, and while it may never be totally environmentally neutral, improvements can be made to reduce the negative impact. At Invesco, these environmental issues are generally high on our agenda when we engage with the senior management of the mining companies in which we have equity holdings. We seek to better understand their views and the steps they are taking to mitigate risks and reduce their carbon footprint.

Mining companies must also have plans for restoring the ecology at the end of the mine’s lifetime. Ethics extend beyond the environment, of course, and mining companies can make a positive impact on addressing socio-economic issues. Given that a productive mine may be in operation for many decades, mining companies will often help develop local housing, transportation, schools, and medical infrastructure. This can have long-term material benefits for emerging economies.

Q4: Does gold have intrinsic value?

While much of gold’s value is linked to its investment characteristics and cultural significance in major jewellery markets such as India and China, the precious metal’s physical properties also make it valuable for use in a range of high-end technology applications, including from the medical and aerospace industries.

Total demand from this sector accounts for just under 10% of annual gold demand, but the amount used has been fairly steady over the past decade, according to data published by the World Gold Council. The electronics sector consumes around 80% of this demand, with gold a critical element in the manufacture of semiconductor chips. In health care, gold nanoparticles are used in many of the diagnostic testing kits, such as those relied on throughout the covid pandemic.

Q5: What role can gold play in a portfolio?

Some investors may add a holding in gold for the same reason they invest in other assets, because they believe the price may rise. This speculation may be driven by the belief that central banks will continue buying gold, or investor interest will increase, or any other factor of supply and demand.

Probably a more likely reason is that investors want to diversify their portfolios or provide a possible cushion against losses in case of rising inflation, stock market volatility, economic uncertainty, or geopolitical risks. Although gold offers no guarantees and can go down even when you expect it to behave differently, historically it has tended to hold up well during sharp equity market downturns.5

Gold can be a useful tool for diversification because its price tends to move differently from most of the other assets in a typical portfolio. Historical evidence shows that the gold price often behaves independently from equities in particular but also bonds to a lesser extent and even a broad basket of commodities. This characteristic means gold can potentially act as a cushion for the rest of the portfolio when other assets are falling in price.

Footnotes

  • Source: Invesco and Bloomberg, comparing selective major benchmarks, all returns in USD, for the 2024 and 2025 calendar years and 2026 year to date to January 31, 2026. For 2024, gold spot price: 27.2%; S&P 500 Index: 25.0%. For 2025, gold spot price: 64.6%; S&P 500 Index: 17.9%. For January 2026, gold spot price: 13.3%; S&P 500 Index: 1.2%. The gold spot price is the midpoint price at which gold can be purchased and received on the current market given current market conditions.

  • Source: World Gold Council, “Gold Investment Rockets in 2025, Setting a New High as Uncertainty Bites,” January 29, 2026.

  • Source: Macrotrends, “Euro Dollar Exchange Rate - EUR USD (1999-2026),” February 25, 2026.

  • Source: World Gold Council, “Why gold in 2025? A cross-asset perspective,” May 2025.

  • Source: World Gold Council, “Why gold in 2025? A cross-asset perspective,” May 2025.

Important Information

NA4625714

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Blockchain is the decentralized, digital public ledger of all cryptocurrency transactions.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Investments in companies located or operating in Greater China are subject to the following risks: nationalization, expropriation, or confiscation of property, difficulty in obtaining and/or enforcing judgments, alteration or discontinuation of economic reforms, military conflicts, and China’s dependency on the economies of other Asian countries, many of which are developing countries.

Commodities may subject an investor to greater volatility than traditional securities such as stocks and bonds and can fluctuate significantly based on weather, political, tax, and other regulatory and market developments.

Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

Fluctuations in the price of gold and precious metals may affect the profitability of companies in the gold and precious metals sector. Changes in the political or economic conditions of countries where companies in the gold and precious metals sector are located may have a direct effect on the price of gold and precious metals.

The health care industry is subject to risks relating to government regulation, obsolescence caused by scientific advances, and technological innovations.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Intrinsic value represents the inherent business value of portfolio holdings during a two- to three-year investment horizon based on their estimates of future cash flow.

Relative value refers to the value of one investment as compared to another.

Stagflation is an economic condition marked by a combination of slow economic growth and rising prices.

Since ordinary brokerage commissions apply for each ETF buy and sell transaction, frequent trading activity may increase the cost of ETFs.

The ICE BofA US 3-Month Treasury Bill Index is made up of one issue of a US Treasury issue that matures in three months. It's purchased when the month starts and is held for one month.

The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, nominal debt issued by the US Treasury.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the global investment-grade, fixed-rate bond market.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers.

The S&P GSCI Index is an unmanaged world production-weighted index composed of the principal physical commodities that are the subject of active, liquid futures markets.

The Bloomberg Commodity Index is a broadly diversified commodity price index.

The MSCI World Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of stocks of developed countries.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of July 24, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

data.title: Market Brief: Is Bitcoin Approaching A Cycle Transition?
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has risen more than 10% over the past seven days, partly driven by renewed macro uncertainty surrounding the Middle East conflict. The recent rebound has revived discussions about whether the market may be transitioning into a new growth phase.

Two long-term charts are drawing attention among Bitcoin observers. One focuses on the time elapsed since the previous all-time high, while the other examines Bitcoin’s performance relative to gold.

Together, they offer a structural lens through which to assess where the market may stand within the broader cycle.

The 23-Month Window

Bitcoin’s previous major bear market bottoms have formed roughly 21 to 23 months after the prior cycle’s all-time high. This timing pattern was visible following the 2013, 2017, and 2021 peaks, even though each cycle unfolded under different macro and liquidity conditions.

We are now approaching that same 23-month mark since the last cycle high. This raises a natural question: could the market once again be nearing a structural turning point, and might a longer-term expansion phase emerge from here?

History suggests that interpretation should remain measured. A time window on its own does not confirm that a bottom has formed. In earlier cycles, stabilization unfolded gradually through extended deleveraging, sentiment resets, and capital reallocation before sustained upside momentum developed.

The current position within this historical time band should therefore be viewed as structural context, not as a definitive signal.

Bitcoin vs Gold: A Relative Reset?

Another chart tracking the BTC/Gold ratio appears to suggest that Bitcoin’s relative performance against gold may be approaching an inflection point.

The ratio compares Bitcoin’s price directly to gold, offering a clear measure of how the two assets perform against each other.

In past cycles, Bitcoin has gone through multi-month periods of underperformance versus gold, often lasting around 14 monthly bars, before regaining relative strength. These phases typically coincided with elevated macro uncertainty and stronger demand for traditional safe-haven assets.

This observation becomes particularly relevant given gold’s strong rally since 2025. As gold has climbed on the back of geopolitical risk, inflation concerns, and sustained safe-haven demand, Bitcoin has gone through a relative consolidation phase against it.

Historically, similar multi-month adjustments in the BTC/Gold ratio have aligned with subsequent shifts in relative performance. As with the time-based cycle window discussed earlier, the ratio now sits within a zone that has previously coincided with structural transitions.

Relative strength cycles, however, are shaped by broader liquidity and macro conditions. The BTC/Gold framework provides structural context, but the direction and pace of any shift depend on how capital flows evolve from here.

What This Suggests

Taken together, the time window and the BTC/Gold adjustment place Bitcoin within a historically meaningful structural zone. Both indicators align with phases that, in prior cycles, coincided with broader transitions in market behavior.

These signals do not define a turning point on their own. They frame the current phase as one where cycle maturity and relative performance are approaching levels previously associated with shifts in market structure.

However, today’s market structure differs from earlier cycles. Institutional participation, derivatives depth, ETF flows, and macro integration may influence how this phase evolves.

Whether the market ultimately enters a renewed expansion phase or continues consolidating will likely depend more on liquidity conditions and macro stability than on the calendar itself.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.
