data.title: Onto Innovation: Why The Risk Is To The Downside
data.description:
Onto Innovation (ONTO), a supplier of equipment for semiconductor manufacturing and packaging, managed to soar higher in past months, especially at the start of 2026. This has resulted in a stock that is worth twice as much as before the rally, even though the stock has pulled back more recently. Moreover, there is something to be gleaned from the recent price action, especially in terms of where the stock reversed course. In fact, there are several reasons why the risk for ONTO could be skewed to the downside, certainly in the wake of the huge rally of the past year.

ONTO looks to have encountered resistance in the charts

A previous article from April 2024 written by myself mentioned how the stock price had rallied higher thanks in large part to investor enthusiasm for potential AI plays, which caused ONTO to come close to almost quadrupling in market cap at the time. On paper, the AI boom ought to increase demand for semiconductors, which works in favor of a supplier of equipment needed to manufacture those chips like ONTO. Yet the rally stood in contrast to earnings results from ONTO, which had actually gotten worse, something that could put the rally in the stock at risk if there was no change. In the end, I opted to rate ONTO a hold in the article.

ONTO experienced a great deal of volatility in the two years that have passed since the article was written. This included a major selloff that wiped out more than half of ONTO’s market cap, but another rally in the stock, especially after August 2025, brought the stock back to where it was prior to the selloff by January 2026.

However, the stock has retreated after failing to move higher for a few weeks, as shown in the above chart. Notice how the stock reached the $220-240 price region as early as January 15 with an intraday high of $227.07, yet ONTO was unable to get past this price region despite months of trying. The closest ONTO got in moving past the $220-240 region was $232.49, immediately after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report.

The multiple failed attempts to get through the aforementioned region in recent weeks is reminiscent of what happened before. The chart above shows how the $220-240 region is a region where a rally by ONTO ended on multiple occasions in the past two years alone. For instance, the stock peaked in this region multiple times in May-July 2024, September-October 2024 and January 2025, similar to what happened in the last two months or so.

WHY ONTO has encountered support

Prior instances were followed by reversals and substantial declines in the stock price after failing to move higher and that appears to be happening again, especially with stocks suffering from the fallout from the war in Iran. If we assume ONTO is in the process of retracing the prior move upwards, which saw ONTO go from a low of $85.88 to a high of $232.49 as shown in the prior chart, then it is worth noting that the stock came close to the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of $85.88 to $232.49, or $176.49, on March 6.

While ONTO closed at $179.72 on March 6 after falling by 8.7% for the day, it went as low as $176.62 on an intaday basis. This is just $0.13 above the previously mentioned support level of $176.49, which could explain why the stock bounced higher to close at $179.72 before the end of the day. This suggests that while resistance is out there in the $220-240 region, ONTO also seems to have found support.

Both resistance and support are present with ONTO in between, but the former looks to be much stronger than the latter considering how long it has been able to prevent the stock from moving beyond a certain point. This suggests that if either is to be broken, support is the one more likely to be it with a move downwards. If or when support does break, then the next possible support level is the 50% Fibonacci retracement, which would take ONTO to around $159.19.

What could drive ONTO lower

As shown earlier, ONTO hit an intraday high of $232.49 on February 20 after the release of the latest report, although it is worth mentioning that the stock closed the day much lower at $216.41. Nonetheless, ONTO posted record numbers in the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report. For instance, revenue reached a record $266.87M in Q4 FY2025, which resulted in non-GAAP net income of $62.26M or $1.26 per share.

On the other hand, this was less than one year ago when ONTO earned $1.51 on revenue of $263.94M in Q4 FY2024. Note also that Q4 FY2025 benefited from the Semilab acquisition, which means the YoY comps could have been worse. In terms of GAAP, ONTO earned $0.21 in Q4 FY2025, less than the $0.98 of a year ago due to a higher combination of operating expenses and taxes. If all the numbers are tabulated, then ONTO ended FY2025 with revenue of $1,005.3M, an increase of 1.8% YoY.

GAAP EPS was $2.78 and non-GAAP EPS was $4.94 in FY2025, both down compared to FY2024 when the numbers stood at $4.06 and $5.34, respectively. Quarterly guidance calls for revenue of $275-285M in Q1 FY2026, which is expected to result in GAAP EPS of $0.74-0.84 and non-GAAP EPS of $1.26-1.36. In comparison, ONTO earned $1.30 and $1.51, respectively, on revenue of $267M in Q1 FY2025. ONTO finished the year with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totaling $639.62M on the balance sheet, partially offset by $17.5M of debt.

Why ONTO could be viewed as potentially overvalued

The numbers from the most recent earnings report were a mixed bag at best, which explains why the stock actually declined by 4% the day after the report. The numbers were not bad, and ONTO did set new records, but they were arguably not good enough when set against the backdrop of a stock that rallied by as much as it did in past months, as shown in the prior charts.

For instance, TTM non-GAAP EPS was $4.94, which accords ONTO a P/E ratio of 36.4x with the stock last closing at $179.72. If we were then to use the methodology of, for instance, Peter Lynch, then ONTO needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of 36.4% in the next 3-5 years in order to be deemed fairly valued at current valuations relative to growth.

The issue here is that ONTO is not growing earnings at the required pace to justify the higher valuations that are the direct result of the rally of the past several months. As shown earlier, earnings have actually decreased. It is possible for ONTO to do better with faster growth in the next five years or FY2025-2030, but recent performance gives ground for doubt, especially if we also consider that EPS only grew from $1.93 to $4.94 in the past five years, or FY2020-2025, which represents a CAGR of 20.68%.

This is well below the 36.4% needed. If ONTO were to grow earnings more in line with what it has demonstrated to be capable of, and EPS grows at a CAGR of 20.68% in the next five years or FY2025-2030, then fair value would be around $102.16. While this may seem far from where the stock is currently at, it is actually what the stock went for as recently as September 2025.

Why the market may be expecting too much of a boost from AI for ONTO

The fact that ONTO soared higher in the Summer of 2025 is not by accident. It happened shortly after many leading AI names announced large spending on related AI infrastructure. This spending on datacenters and so on is perceived to increase semiconductor demand, which in turn boosts demand for production capacity and by extension the equipment needed for production, including the metrology, inspection and other tools provided by ONTO.

However, more growth may have been priced in than ONTO may be able to deliver. The boom in AI has already been underway for several years, and while ONTO has definitely reaped benefits from AI, the impact has not been all that large, as shown once more by the latest report. The latest numbers, for instance, were achieved with an AI boom already long underway.

ONTO for its part does not call for a huge acceleration in growth compared to what has already been seen, something that needs to happen to justify the appreciation in the stock of recent months and the elevated valuations it led to. The current outlook from ONTO, for example, calls for revenue to grow by 12-14% YoY in the first half of FY2026. From the Q4 FY2025 earnings call:

“revenue in Q2 is expected to surpass $300 million, which will result in 12% to 14% core growth in the first half of 2026 as compared to the second half of 2025.”

This is not much better than the CAGR of 12.56% revenue grew by in the past five years. If revenue stays at this level, in the low-to-mid teens, then earnings could grow in the low twenties, which is by how much earnings grew when revenue grew by 12.56% on average. All this raises doubts whether AI can drive earnings to such an extent in the coming years that it justifies paying a multiple in the high thirties.

It’s also possible all the announced spending may not even materialize. A major catalyst occurred in the Summer of 2025 when companies like OpenAI, Nvidia (NVDA) and Oracle (ORCL) announced investments potentially reaching hundreds of billions, but, more recently, there are signs Nvidia, for instance, is having second thoughts about how much they will or should invest in OpenAI.

Much if not all of the rally following the Summer of 2025 was predicated on AI, but what was announced and what ends up being implemented may not actually turn out to be the same. Already companies like Oracle are putting a heavy burden on their balance sheet with all that capex spending. There are no assurances ONTO will get much more from AI that what it got in the last couple of years.

Investor takeaways

ONTO is a stock that has more than doubled its market cap since the Summer, but there is reason to think the stock price would be much lower if not for AI. A lot of deals were announced in the AI space during the Summer of 2025, all of which raised expectations as to how much capex spending on AI there will be in the coming years.

This in turn is expected to lead to increased demand for equipment from suppliers like ONTO, particularly in the areas of metrology and packaging. This is reflected in how much future growth has been priced into the stock with the increase in the stock price in the months following Summer 2025. However, there is reason to doubt whether earnings will actually grow fast enough in the coming years to justify the higher valuations that resulted from the increase in the price of the stock.

The latest outlook calls for revenue to grow by 12-14% in the next two earnings reports, which is not much better than what ONTO managed in the past five years, and that is with a recent acquisition adding to revenue growth. At this expected pace of growth in revenue, earnings are likely to grow in the low twenties, but that also suggests ONTO is overvalued at current prices, as elaborated on earlier in the article.

Being overvalued relative to future growth could be the catalyst that takes the stock price lower. Furthermore, the charts suggest the stock is more likely to go lower than higher after failing to get past resistance in the $220-240 region. This region has proven to be hard to get by, judging by how many times the stock reversed course to head lower in the past two years. It is not impossible for ONTO to break resistance that has held its ground for the last two years, but it is more likely that ONTO goes lower, even with the presence of support.

I am neutral on ONTO with all the above in mind. ONTO has done very well as far as the stock is concerned, but the risk is to the downside at this point after the run-up in the price of the stock. The latest results and the outlook suggest that much more future growth has been priced into the stock than ONTO is likely to deliver in the coming years. If ONTO is to justify present valuations, it needs to grow faster, especially in terms of earnings. If it does not and growth stays where it is at or close to it, the stock is going down.

data.title: Aclara Resources: A Long Term Investment In Brazil's Rare Earths Sectors
data.description:
Aclara Resources Inc. (ARAAF) is a mining company that's looking toward the future of energy based on REE (rare earth elements). The key REE minerals are increasingly necessary for both the automotive industry and the consumer electronics sector.

Aclara is making a strong investment in these operations, especially in extracting mines located in Brazil (the Carina Project) and Chile (the Penco Module Project). But the mine I like the most is the Brazilian one, because it can produce 175 tons of DyTb (dysprosium and terbium) annually, more than three times the 50 tons of the Chilean project. DyTb are heavy chemistral elements within the REE group, known as HREE, which are the most resistant to high temperature technologies. They are also very difficult to find and produce compared to LREE.

Besides the mentioned projects in Brazil and Chile, Aclara needs a plant of chemistral separation plant for the extracted materials, which is getting developed in Louisiana, with an estimated capital expenditure of $280 million. The investment has $46 million in state fiscal support, as part of the country's strategy to benefit the production of REE. This plant, which plays a key role in the supply chain of the company, is expected to begin operations in mid-2028.

The company is still in the project execution phase, securing financing from its main shareholders, and expects to make its first sales in 2028. The main shareholder is the Hochschild group, which owns almost 57% of the shares, while the Chilean group CAP holds 10%. The rest of the capital is divided among minority shareholders. I believe Aclara has huge potential in the long term in Brazil’s REE sector, but I also believe it is not mature enough to buy yet, because its revenue is still far off in time.

The global demand for rare earth elements

I believe REE is already changing the outlook for future energy, and they could be fundamental for the following sectors: EV and consumer electronics. Moreover, these sectors already account for the majority of demand for NdPr (neodymium and praseodymium), key LREE magnets for EVs and for technological devices used daily, from computers and smartphones to video games and household appliances.

There are other important sectors, such as industry, defense, and renewable energy, that are demanding NdPr. For those who are not very familiar with these terms, they are two elements of the 17 REEs that exist in the world. REEs are on the whole earth's crust, but their defining characteristic is their scarcity in large quantities, especially HREEs, which also require more investment for productive use. Next, you'll see a table with the main uses of each element.

What I found in Aclara's projections is that its demand will grow faster than supply, creating a bottleneck that opens great business opportunities. Just as AI data centers will demand more energy than current electrical systems can provide, I believe REEs are playing a similar role.

DyTb, for example, is expected to face a supply deficit of 1600 tons during 2030, to which Aclara estimates it will contribute with a production of 225 tons between Penco and Carina. For NdPr demand, the situation is very similar, with a supply gap of 57 kilotonnes by 2032, driven largely by the surge in EVs, since NdPr will be a crucial input. To this future global demand, must also add the price effect, and this is where geopolitics comes into play, because very few countries have these resources within their borders. NdPr oxide prices have risen over the past few months due to restrictions on exports from China, as you can see in the following Reuters image.

This increase, which reached $123, is a very good opportunity for Western miners that are already operating in this market, but that isn't yet the case for Aclara, which is still in a development phase. Companies such as MP Materials (MP) or Critical Metals (CRML), which are more operationally established, showed a very good performance so far in 2026. I believe there is other very good news for the American government, which is planning to provide a subsidy to these companies if NdPr prices fall below $110.

Although the current price doesn't directly affect Aclara, it was very helpful to better understand the geopolitical context. Because these prices are driven by Chinese decisions, I looked into the global supply by country. And China was not only the lucky one, but also the most productive country. I said it was lucky because it owns 54% of the proven reserves, but it is also the leader in production, representing 68% of the global market. It is an even greater dominance than in steel, where China accounts for 54% of the global supply.

The U.S. is the next largest producer after China, although it only owns 2.3% of the total reserves. But my attention is on Brazil, which holds 14% of global reserves and has huge potential because its production has been virtually null. At first glance, the Brazilian market might seem unattractive, but the market rewards the future, not the past. Next, I'll explain in more detail why I am optimistic about the role of miners in Brazil’s REE. But just to give you a sense of my view: if the U.S. produces only 11.4% of global supply while holding just 2.3% of the reserves, how much could Brazil produce with 14% of the reserves?

Brazil's potential in rare earth elements

I believe the potential of Brazil’s natural conditions in REE is an undeniable reality, the same way as the Orinoco oil fields for Venezuela or Vaca Muerta for Argentina. However, realizing this energy potential requires large investments, legal certainty, and a stable political and economic environment.

The problem for Brazil is that none of these conditions was successful until now. Local credit financing is not enough to exploit renewable energy resources, and monetary policy has kept the benchmark interest rate well above inflation. The rate is approximately 15%, while expected inflation is below 5%. Although Brazil's Central Bank estimates that an interest rate cut cycle may begin.

But despite these structural difficulties, I have found some signals that Brazil’s REE market is changing. First of all, exports reached a record level in the first half of 2025, totaling $7.5 million. This is still a low number, but it represented a great jump compared to the $705k in the same period the previous year.

The impetus comes from the fact that the Brazilian government has already launched a strategic plan to support the mining sector through the BNDES bank and in partnership with the U.S. to finance REE production with $565 million. As you can see, this is a larger amount than exports in just one semester; that's why I said some significant investments are needed to get this started. Perhaps these aren’t the kinds of investments I’m used to reading from hyperscalers in data centers, but they are significant relative to Brazil.

Aclara is focused on the Serra Verde deposit, located in Goias, and the mining companies that operate in Brazil extract a large part of the resources there for export. There is the Carina Project, for which Aclara estimates a CAPEX of $680 million. Serra Verde is a key geological formation with an estimated lifespan of 25 years, although it's not the only one: the states of Minas Gerais and Bahia also have advanced exploration and production projects.

However, Serra Verde is different because it is the only deposit outside China that could extract HREE from ionic clay and not from hard rock. And this simplifies the extraction process. And once again, no deposit is exactly the same as another, not only in Brazil but in the whole West. Basically, what I'm seeing is a key to unlocking Brazil's potential. Those who invest and produce in Sierra Verde (which I estimate will be many more companies) will have a competitive advantage in the REE market.

The company has an MoU in Goiás, which shortens the permitting timeline and estimates that construction can begin in Q2 2026. The company is also taking advantage of the signal that the U.S. will provide additional funding to support the project during the construction phase. Operations are expected to start in 2028, producing 175 tons of DyTb.

Penco Module

Aclara’s other major investment is in Penco, a project located in the Biobío region of Chile, where it expects to reach a production of 50 tons of DyTb once the plant becomes operational in mid-2028. Its CAPEX is lower than Serra Verde's ($130 million), but the Chilean project remains important because it complements the future operational strength of the company. You know, you shouldn't put all your eggs in one basket. My enthusiasm is then in Brazil, and it will be the focus of my future investment (if everything goes well) during the next quarters. But any new production is good to add.

The Penco Module covers 600 hectares, has an extraction life of 14 years, and has the same advantage as the Carina project in terms of local government support. The project was recognized by the Chilean government itself, as one of the 25 key plans for the country’s mining development. This gained further relevance with the election of right-wing President José Antonio Kast, aligned with pro-market policies, who defeated the left-wing incumbent Gabriel Boric last December.

Financing and cash flow

Since the company is still in the development phase, there is no billing or projected revenue yet. I saw some analysts project positive FCF after taxes only from 2029 onwards, which makes sense because operations are expected to start in mid-2028.

Based on its latest reported financials from Q3 2025, the cash position increased YoY during the first nine months of 2025, rising from $25.4 million to $27.1 million. Exploration and asset valuation expenses rose sharply during the year, reaching $23.1 million, but this was offset in the same period by a substantial capital contribution of $25 million. Cash flow would have deteriorated a lot without this contribution.

Given that all the investment projects totaled $1.22 billion through 2028 (Carina Project, Penco Module, Louisiana Separation Project, and Metals and Alloys Project), I anticipate that further rounds of capital financing will be necessary, either through equity or through debt financing. I believe Aclara has an advantage that has not yet been used: debt, which is practically zero. Meanwhile, operating liabilities were $7.8 million, which I don't think will complicate their balance sheet. I foresee that debt will be a resource to be used in the short term, and perhaps it will be the most necessary thing to accelerate the projects.

Volatility risks in Latin America

Although I believe any REE investment could be a great opportunity, Aclara is still in the development phase, so it faces particular risks that could delay its operations. Given that the estimates I presented show a first major peak in demand closer to 2030, any delays in operations could negatively impact the stock.

Besides, it is important to closely monitor both projects in Brazil and Chile, which means taking into account political and economic factors, typically unstable, because they are Latin American countries. Brazil, for example, has elections in October, where the current leftist president, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, will seek reelection. Flavio Bolsonaro, son of former right-wing president Jair Bolsonaro, has emerged as a leading candidate in the opposition, and a victory could boost optimism in financial markets.

However, although Lula didn’t clash much with Trump, there was a sort of agreement on REE during their last meeting. In other words, Brazil's REE potential is not necessarily tied to political decisions. In Chile, the political outlook appears more stable following Kast's arrival to the presidency, where he will have to serve until 2029.

Summary

I really like companies with long-term investment plans focused on the energy of the future. In my opinion, renewable energy sources will be a key part of the future energy mix and are already shaping the geopolitical competition between the U.S. and China. Aclara is a mining company with a smart, strategic plan, especially for leveraging Brazil's rare earth potential. That said, I think it's still too early to call it a buy, so I'm just keeping an eye on it to see how it develops in the coming quarters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Enphase: The 'Sneaky AI Thesis' Played Out; Now It's Time To Step Aside
data.description:
Enphase (ENPH) has finally seen a bounce. The first quarter is expected to mark a bottom for solar demand with sequential improvement throughout the year. Investors might also be beginning to wonder if rising electricity prices due to AI data centers might prove to be a tailwind for solar installations. This is an intriguing angle, which I have made before, but I am now of the view that it is fully priced into the stock. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

ENPH Stock Price

I last covered ENPH in December, where I rated the stock on the “sneaky AI thesis.” The stock has soared 37% since.

While management looks confident that the bottom is in, I view the recent rally as a good chance to step aside.

ENPH Stock Key Metrics

ENPH is a critical player in the solar market, known for its microinverters. These microinverters, in ENPH’s opinion, are more reliable than string inverters due to not having one single source of failure. That assertion may have been called into question given Tesla’s (TSLA) great success in the space using string inverters, but for the most part, ENPH is still able to command premium pricing due to having one of the highest quality products on the market.

ENPH has begun shipping its newest IQ9N microinverter for the commercial market, which is a milestone as it allows the company to support larger commercial buildings. ENPH has historically made most of its business in residential installations, and this pronounced entrance into the commercial markets may be a key growth driver over the coming years.

In its most recent quarter, ENPH saw revenue decline 10.3% YoY to $343.3 million, coming in at the high end of guidance of between $310 million and $350 million. The decline was expected given that the prior quarter had seen a lot of pull-forward in demand due to the anticipated expiration of solar credits. While non-GAAP operating margins did contract by over 800 bps, ENPH was still profitable even on a GAAP basis.

ENPH ended the quarter with $1.5 billion of cash and investments versus $1.2 billion of debt.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up to $300 million in revenue, representing a 15.8% YoY decline.

On the conference call, management gave expectations for the first quarter to be “the low point for underlying demand with improvement expected through 2026.” Management qualified that stance with expectations for improvement driven by their “new financing options, such as the prepaid leases,” rising utility rates (perhaps due to the buildout of AI data centers), as well as potentially declining interest rates. While investors can be forgiven if they are impatient of hearing the same calls for a bottom for so long now, I do think that this time is different given the multitude of negative drivers causing the poor recent results (which may give way to easier comparables thereafter).

Is ENPH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, ENPH traded at around 20x this year’s earnings. I note that these are non-GAAP numbers. GAAP earnings came in at 55% lower than non-GAAP earnings in 2025. On a GAAP basis, I estimate that ENPH is trading at just under 40x earnings.

To be fair, the difference between non-GAAP and GAAP earnings is likely to diminish as the business recovers. Consensus estimates call for the company to see a return to double-digit growth following this year.

The recent rally, honestly, was unexpected to me, given the timing. It has brought many of my recent calls to positive territory, but I am still significantly underwater on calls like the one I made in early 2025. I do not typically like to give up on holdings, but in this case, my initial thesis has proven incorrect, as I acknowledged in May. I had underestimated the amount of cyclicality in this business as well as the reliance on regulatory credits. That cyclicality is important to keep in mind when assessing the valuation and potential growth prospects. I no longer expect the company to see a return to the incredible growth rates of the past. Between a potentially difficult economy, ongoing competition from TSLA, and the expiration of residential solar credits, growth is likely to be muted at least over the next 2 to 3 years. The stock’s 16x forward earnings multiple for 2027 might not look obviously expensive, but I suspect that it is not offering enough of a margin of safety in the event the company faces more cyclicality, perhaps due to the same AI-driven job displacement fears that have been causing volatility across the stock market. While it is possible that ENPH sees some tailwinds from rising electricity prices, it remains to be seen if this rise proves enough to make solar installations more attractive to strained consumers. The stock still has some upside to my prior target of 7x sales in a bullish thesis, which keeps me from downgrading this to a sell rating. But I see limited reasons to count on upside in the near to medium term, as there are no visible catalysts to drive enough growth to move the needle. I would be more interested in the stock again if it traded down to 10x to 12x 2027e earnings, or $27 to $32 per share.

ENPH Stock Conclusion

I do believe that the first quarter will prove to be the low point for the company, with improvement thereafter. The company has arguably faced a perfect storm of bad luck, but the advantage is that things should get better from here. The company deserves recognition for maintaining GAAP profitability even during these low points, though I have clearly underestimated the inherent cyclicality in past coverage. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

data.title: AutoZone Q2 Earnings: New Store Openings Drive Growth, But Weak Demand Weighs
data.description:
AutoZone (AZO) is a retailer of automotive replacement parts and accessories. The firm has business in the United States, Mexico, and Brazil.

I started covering the company last December with an initial neutral rating, with consumer sentiment and valuation being the key points of concern. Since my writing, AZO slightly underperformed the broader market (SPY) and performed approximately in line with the consumer discretionary sector - despite a major drawdown in late December.

The aim of my article today is to give an updated view on the firm, taking into account the most recent earnings results. I will also give an update on the macroeconomic environment, as well as on the valuation metrics.

Earnings

In the most recent quarter, AutoZone's EPS came in at $27.63, beating analyst estimates by $0.34, but declining 2.3% compared to the prior year. The price reaction was negative after the beat, however, driven by the worse-than-expected top-line results. Revenue came in at $4.27B, representing an 8.1% growth year-over-year, but missing expectations by $40 million.

Let us dive a bit deeper into the results and see what the key driving factors are.

Income statement

First of all, when looking at the net sales, we have to understand that the growth is coming largely from expansion/new store sales. The firm increased its store count by 64 compared to the prior year. This, on one hand, is good, as the firm is executing its foreseen growth strategy and increasing its footprint both domestically and internationally. On the other hand, it still shows that macroeconomic headwinds are putting downward pressure on the demand. In contrast to the net sales growth, same store sales only grew by 3.3%, coming in below analyst estimates. When looking at the growth geographically (in constant currency), it is definitely promising, however, that the growth is broad-based, meaning that both the domestic and international segments grew. I generally like it when a firm has a geographically diversified revenue stream.

Looking ahead, I believe that the demand for AZO's products is strongly controlled by the financial health of the consumer. As long as consumer sentiment is poor, the demand is not likely to increase substantially.

Another key driver of the demand for AZO's products is the portion of EVs. EVs normally need less maintenance and fewer replacement parts than internal combustion engine cars, potentially hurting AZO's business. Over the past years, the share of electric car sales increased significantly, not only in the United States, but globally as well.

In terms of profitability, the results are also not very convincing. The gross margin shrank by 137 basis points compared to the same period in the prior year, while the operating margin contracted by 154 basis points. The decline in net income was as much as 3.9%, which was slightly better on a per share basis, due to the reduced number of shares. The driver of the gross margin contraction was the non-cash LIFO charge, while the investments supporting the company's growth initiatives put downward pressure on the operating income. The non-cash LIFO charge is essentially a result of AZO's bookkeeping practices, meaning that the newest, most expensive inventory is sold first, making the cost of goods sold higher.

Balance sheet

Let us look at the balance sheet now.

First of all, the company's inventory increased. This is partially a result of the new store opening, and partially a result of inflation. As a result of the higher inventory, the firm's efficiency deteriorated somewhat, as shown by the decreased inventory turns. On the other hand, I have to highlight that AZO slightly managed to reduce its debt.

Return to shareholders

AZO remained committed to returning value to its shareholders through share buybacks. In the most recent quarter, the company spent $310.8 million on buybacks. With this, they have about $1.4 billion remaining under the current authorization.

All in all, I believe that AZO's growth both domestically and internationally is impressive, but the macroeconomic headwinds, as well as the deteriorating profitability, are concerning. For these reasons, I do not believe that a buy rating can be warranted from a fundamental point of view now.

Valuation

To assess the valuation, let us look at the same set of traditional price multiples as we did in my previous analysis.

Back then - just like now - all the multiples indicated an overvaluation compared to both the sector median and the historic averages. I believed that this premium was not justified, and I still believe now that the shares are overvalued.

If we narrow down the peer group to only a few key players in the industry, the picture looks somewhat better, but in absolute terms, I do not believe that these valuation metrics can be justified.

For these reasons, I cannot get bullish on the firm from a valuation point of view either.

Conclusions

Despite beating analyst estimates on the bottom line, the firm's EPS declined year-over-year. While revenue grew compared to the prior year, results came in below analyst expectations, prompting a negative price reaction after the announcement.

Sales growth was mainly driven by new store openings. Same store sales contributed only 3.1% in constant currency to the total growth.

Profitability deteriorated compared to the prior year. Margins shrank, driven by the non-cash LIFO charge, and the investments supporting AZO's growth.

The firm remains unattractive from a valuation point of view, especially when we consider the macroeconomic headwinds caused by the poor consumer sentiment and the gradually increasing share of EVs, globally.

For these reasons, I maintain my previous hold rating.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Earnings Are Fine
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events, as well as the top-down, providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of.

Market Action

BDCs finished the week in the red after a sharp fall on Friday. The catalyst for the drop appears to be the news of a bankruptcy of a UK mortgage finance company, MFS.

There are a few interesting parallels so far. One, this appears to be a case of fraud and double-pledging of assets, similar to the earlier saga with First Brands and Tricolor. Two, the companies on the hook appear to be a combination of banks (Barclays, Jefferies) as well as asset-backed finance investors such as Atlas SP Partners (owned by Apollo) and Castlelake LP. This was also what we saw in the cases of First Brands and Tricolor, with one difference: there were some small positions across a handful of BDCs in First Brands loans.

What's also interesting is that the size of the MFS default appears to be fairly low given how much noise it has made. The total liability amount of the company appears to be smaller than either First Brands or Tricolor.

All in all, the MFS story is not only less material but is also even further removed from BDC lending than First Brands/Tricolor which was almost entirely a non-BDC story.

Market Commentary

This was a busy week for BDC Q4 earnings reports. Outside of a couple of poor results from TCPC and FSK, two BDCs that we have avoided, the numbers were just fine, if not spectacular.

BBDC delivered a good Q4 result with a 2.3% total NAV return (2.9% on a valuation-adjusted basis based on the latest discount of 21%). NII fell from Q3; however, it wasn’t far off the midpoint of the previous Q4/Q1 numbers. The company repurchased shares, which is good to see in today’s environment, adding a penny to the NAV. It also set up a new $30m share repurchase plan for 2026.

It is looking to rotate the portfolio further towards interest-earning assets, which should support NII and the NAV. The stock has been somewhat resilient to the software theme, outperforming the sector year-to-date. The allocation to software is around 14%, or about two-thirds of the typical BDC.

NCDL delivered a solid result with a +1.8% total NAV return, +2.4% on a valuation-adjusted basis, which works out stronger than the median in our coverage as well as popular names like TSLX, ARCC and others. NII came in at $0.44, a penny above the Q3 result. The company reduced the dividend from a $0.45 distribution to a base of $0.36 and a supplemental $0.04.

Non-accruals stayed low at 0.5%. All of the company’s debt is floating-rate, which allowed interest expense to fall by 0.4% over the quarter to 6.2%, preserving the net margin. It’s disappointing the company did not repurchase shares during the quarter, which would have boosted the performance.

BXSL reported a good result with a 2% total NAV return for Q4 and a 2.2% valuation-adjusted figure, slightly below the median in our coverage. Relative to history, the performance was not very impressive, primarily due to around 0.9% of unrealized depreciation on a handful of names. Non-accruals rose to about 0.5%, still less than half of the sector median level. The number of names on the watchlist fell, however.

Over the last 7 years, the portfolio has had zero net realized losses, so there is a good chance that the unrealized depreciation doesn’t result in a realized loss and gets written back up as BXSL manages the affected companies. There were 3 names during the quarter that were previously marked in the low 90s that have repaid or will repay this quarter at par.

Stance and Takeaways

The BDC market remains very jittery and scared of any negative headlines, however remote they are to the sector. Friday's drop of 3.6% on average due to a bankruptcy of a relatively small and unrelated UK mortgage provider speaks to how scared the sector is of its own shadow. So far the underlying performance has been good, if not great. We continue to like names like BCSF, NCDL, OTF and a number of others.

data.title: Avoiding Ares Management Corporation And Ares Capital As Private Credit Headwinds Persist
data.description:
Ares Management Corporation (ARES) and Ares Capital (ARCC) were not immune to the Private Credit carnage last week. ARES is among the worst performing Private Credit advisor equities year-to-date. Yet ARCC’s approximate 6% discount to its year-end $19.94 Net Asset Value (“NAV”) is a far richer relative valuation than OWL’s OBDC, FS/KKR’s FSK, and Blackstone’s BXSL.

Ares’ 2026 equity performance paired with its Business Development Company (“BDC”) trading still trading near NAV among a heavily discounted peer group presents a market paradox. This article will address the market paradox as informed by my background in 1940 Act companies and the dynamic market environment for Private Credit and its vehicles.

The Week In Private Credit

The latest week in Private Credit finished with BlackRock (BLK) limiting Q1 redemptions of its flagship private credit fund. As recently as Tuesday, the concerns had only been the requests for record redemptions. And to be clear, BlackRock's $1.2 billion request brought the total among two high-profile non-traded funds revealed in the week to a clean $5 billion. But Blackstone’s massive $82 billion non-traded BCRED had met all of its record-high redemption requests

BlackRock did not expand its buyback to meet such redemptions. Market observers appear alarmed, but any surprise should be limited. The N2 (prospectus/registration statement) for the fund reads, “Under our share repurchase program, to the extent we offer to repurchase shares in any particular quarter, we intend to limit the number of shares to be repurchased to no more than 5% of our outstanding Common Shares as of the last day of the immediately preceding quarter.” While any potential effect on BlackRock’s brand is uncertain, the redemption went as clearly stated.

Of course, BlackRock could have chosen to make an exception, as Blackstone did. I am visualizing a Saturday Night Live parody where a black-colored stone and a black-colored rock make different business choices. Forgive my humor. To be clear, Blackstone was not required to make an exception to its own 5% quarterly gate. But right now, most of our investing population is just learning about Private Credit, including many who bought these BDCs absent much research. Investors are reacting to business decisions emotionally, and that may continue. Investors are quickly learning more about private credit because of market action.

So what about Ares and ARCC?

Until Friday, it might have been kind to analogize Ares being to Private Credit what BlackRock was to broad asset management. BlackRock is touted as the world's largest asset manager. Ares is a dominant player in Private Credit. Their BDC has traded among the best relative valuations, and of Ares’ $623 billion in AUM, $407 billion is in the credit space. While the analogy may be bittersweet today, both have leadership status they risk losing. And Ares’ prospects are closely tied to Private Credit.

Ares now trades around 18 times estimated forward earnings. And at first glance, forward earnings do not sound like a pie in the sky. For the most recent quarter, after-tax realized income per share was $1.45. But Ares's proportional business in Private Credit certainly may warrant caution if the money flowing into private credit slows or reverses. Precaution on those forward earnings may be warranted.

Ares, similar to how its BDC trades can be viewed as expensive if its earnings were to falter. Its book value has been reported at $12.68 per share. Trading at 8.7 times book is not generally regarded as a value stock, to say the least.

Ares is valued like a growth stock. It can grow its private credit AUM two ways. It can sustain its market share in a growing market, or it can increase its market share. Separately, upward or downward pressure on advisory services pricing can prove highly consequential to its fee related earnings. That brings us to the value proposition of private equity vehicles managed by Ares and others. Will there be downward pressure on advisory pricing?

ARCC and Private Credit BDCs in general

My primary competency is 1940 Act companies, inclusive of closed-end funds (“CEFs”) and business development companies (“BDCs”) such as ARCC.

ARCC shareholders appear emotionally convinced they have the best advisor in Private Credit. Yes, I read the comments. Also, ARCC traded at a premium to NAV for most of 2019 and that premium has been pretty consistent outside of the COVID crisis ever since. I do not know whether the present 6% discount is a blip, or whether change is underway. Either way, Ares may be the best advisor. The market appears to have said so for six years. While I also don’t care to portray such as justified, I absolutely tip my cap to Ares’ branding.

But ARCC finds itself now factually trading at a discount, albeit small. This is math. Also, ARCC currently pays a 48 cent quarterly distribution. $1.92 annually, if sustained, would represent 10.2% annually. Of course, where that dividend goes over time is uncertain but related to Ares, Private Credit dynamics, and much more.

What are the costs of investing in ARCC for that 10.2% yield?

ARCC pays Ares a management fee and incentive fee, similar to other BDCs. When these companies register, the fees they will charge are detailed in the N2 filed with the SEC. ARCC’s document clearly specifies an incentive fee equal to 20% of net investment income, subject to a 1.75% quarterly hurdle rate and a catch-up provision. The income incentive fee is applicable to PIK income, and there is separately a 20% of realized capital gains incentive fee (net of realized/unrealized losses), although that is generally less relevant as realized gains are harder for an advisor to achieve than income. Both incentive fees are on top of a 1.5% base management fee.

ARCC has always had a rich management contract. Such incentive-laden contracts are structurally similar to hedge funds in that the advisor gets not just the management fee, but a portion of the returns too. Such a structure can be perceived as expensive in fixed-income products. But good returns have a way of desensitizing investors to expenses—while they last. The relative valuation, usually above NAV has inferred shareholders have not been sensitive to taking all the investment risk, while sharing the returns borne through that risk. Maybe shareholders would be equally gratuitous if total returns deteriorated. I do not know what the future holds. But I am skeptical of shareholders ongoing appetite for the 20% incentive fees if this change of tide lasts much longer. Pain brings an attention to detail. I believe shareholders will increasingly cost-compare BDCs and the asset class of private credit BDCs to more simple income alternatives, just as I do.

15% and 17.5% incentive fees are increasingly common in the space versus ARCC’s 20%. Is that still expensive, perhaps? Many funds with 15% and 17.5% incentive fees trade at large discounts, so cost is not the only factor at play. I strongly believe Ares does an exceptional job of branding, but I digress.

Separately, it is important to consider the cost of leverage. ARCC issues debt separately from lending to borrowers. This is commonly referred to as leverage, and obviously that leverage comes at a cost. For example, ARCC issued $750 million in 5.250% unsecured notes due in 2031 about two months ago. To ARCC’s credit, they got better terms than peers. But it is important to realize the buyers of those or any other ARCC notes are protected by the 1940 Act. ARCC common shareholders have no such protection.

I choose not to aggregate an expense ratio of any of the BDCs I cover for fear of missing a waiver, a reimbursement, or otherwise failing to compare each BDC or BDC ETF on an apples-to-apples basis. My only point is that the costs of publicly traded BDC wrappers are high. Readers may choose to use an AI or their information source (i.e., Bloomberg) to aggregate the total costs with a like-apples basis of their choosing.

Bridging the gap between gross and net returns may explain the motivation behind the recent institutional buyers of $1.4B of Private Credit assets at 99.7 cents on the dollar from Blue Owl (OWL). Those institutional buyers kept the asset class but got out of the structural wrapper.

What are the risks of investing in any BDC for a 10.2% yield?

There is no substitute for reading “Risk Factors” in an N2 but I will highlight some risks I think are essential to focus on.

A BDC’s yield can change. I compare each to a fixed 8% yield that I can get with zero underlying expenses, zero yield variation, and essentially no default risk. This expert tutorial may help many understand the far safer and fee-free alternative of a 1940 Act-protected CEF preferred. With 1940 Act Protected Preferreds, I have an expense ratio of absolutely zero and just collect interest directly from my exchange-traded security (i.e., HFRO.PR.A) like a direct bond investor. No advisor is making a penny off of me. That is where most of my fixed income investment goes. If I were to buy a BDC like ARCC yielding 10.2%, I would anticipate getting about 2% extra yield at the time of purchase. But if there was one 20% cut to the distribution, or two 10% cuts, I’d be roughly equal on yield to my far safer option. And larger than 20% cuts can be announced by any BDC with any quarter’s earnings.

NAV can absorb losses. Private Credit can experience defaults. Assets can be remarked due to liquidity issues, market trends, or increasing risks. Or the BDC can simply fail to cover its planned distribution with income less expenses. Each of these can have a negative impact on the NAV, which BDCs report quarterly.

Market premiums can become market discounts, and market discounts can grow. I believe this will be a prevailing trend as the cost structure of BDCs is more frequently analyzed. I previously mentioned institutions apparently bridging the gap between gross and net returns as a hypothetical motivation behind the recent institutional buyers of $1.4B of Private Credit assets at 99.7 cents on the dollar from Blue Owl. If a cost-sensitive era is upon us in Private Credit, that would not appear to be a good catalyst for a BDC with a 20% incentive. If any wrapper requires forever paying 15% of the returns to an advisor, I see the assets as worth 15% less inside the wrapper. I apply the same model whether the advisor gets 10%, 17.5%, or any other portion of the income paid as an incentive. That is before considering supply/demand issues, as discount momentum may affect investor tastes.

Most importantly, these publicly traded BDCs never have to buy shares back unless a Board of Directors so chooses. Discounts can get big when a volume of shareholders wants to exit the BDC and the board chooses not to buy back shares in NAV accretive transactions. As discounts grow, the momentum can be quite painful. Historically, when things get especially bad, activism becomes likely. Activism is not a valid exit consideration for a BDC trading anywhere near NAV as ARCC does.

Conclusion

By discussing these costs and risks, it may sound like I am anti-private credit. I want to be clear: I do see private credit as having a place in fixed-income portfolios over long periods of time. It is the wrappers and their pricing (both fees and market discount) to which I apply a very keen eye. I feel it is increasingly important to see an exit strategy and/or to only rent these vehicles at periods when selling pressure has reached short-term peaks. That’s a hard game to play, and I really do not have any reason to expect we are there with ARCC in particular.

I do not argue with what ARES and ARCC shareholders believe about the advisor's competency. That said, ARES's performance of late suggests that with both the advisor and BDC being current leaders in the space, they both have more to lose (growth expectations, relative valuation) than their respective peers today.

Whatever the future might hold, we should all understand exactly what we own and what the pros and cons of each are. For me, private credit plays a pretty small role when I do use its wrappers at all (as I do now). Know the risks of each investment we make and consider the excess returns we are attempting to generate in comparison to less risky investments.

I strongly believe that for the lion’s share of my allocation to fixed income, it is much wiser to invest in the 0% expense and essentially default risk-free 1940 Act Protected CEF preferreds discussed here. I say that openly and honestly in spite of one of my seven largest positions being a Private Credit advisor. And I think we can all agree that, for better or worse, Private Credit advisors are well compensated.

Action idea: If I had over 5% of my capital in private credit inclusive of ARCC, I would start slowly transitioning more than half of the ARCC to the far safer HFRO.PR.A and keep the rest. Right now, the 1940 Act protected safer choice offers just 2% less than ARCC’s anticipated annual yield.
